title: The Left has Failed Iran
subtitle: Socialism's Fetish for US Aggression
author: rafaelholmberg
raw_article:
In a recent article for The Guardian, Nasrin Parvaz, a survivor of the Iranian Regime’s long tradition of torturing women, describes the perpetual sense of defeat felt by Iranians. The article is a short but tragic reflection on the deadlock Iran currently faces, on the absence of any escape routes that do not simply return us to the problem itself. It is clear, as Parvaz writes, that Iran is confronting a series of empty alternatives, each worse than the next: the election of Mojtaba Khamenei as Supreme Leader (himself even more conservative than his father), a frantic bombing campaign by the US and Israel which is likely to strengthen sympathies for the same clerical rule that the protests sought to extinguish, or a return to the same US-puppet Pahlavi monarchism originally responsible for the crisis of 1979. Neither of these alternatives are a terminal point. They point to the old Leninist problem of opposed political alternatives which ultimately justify and bolster each other.

Nowhere, however, either in Parvaz's article or elsewhere, is the complicity of the left in this catastrophe mentioned. Today’s left does not so much want to prevent atrocity as it wants to narcissistically reflect on it from its own impotent passivity. The left was silent as the Regime was murdering tens of thousands of democratic protesters. The only option, in lieu of a serious liberatory project by the international left, was for an opportunist, authoritarian intervention by the US and Israel. As is always the case, this was an intervention with no interest in actually liberating Iranians on humanitarian grounds.

Mapping the Iranian protests was difficult for many leftists: since 1979, Iran has been the avatar of anti-capitalist, neo-colonialist politics, an opposition movement to the globalised US-Empire of late capitalism. Confused by the Islamic Revolution, and with an irrational shift from the War on Communism to the War on Terror (which seems to equate anti-capitalist communism with Islamic fundamentalism), the left hoped that they had found new ally. This was even helped by the fact that Iran funds what many on the left like to see as today's anti-imperialist resistance movements (Hamas, Hezbollah etc.). This binary narrative meant that the left were immobilised when the 'anti-imperialist Regime' itself began murdering its own population. The left lacked the basic coordinates to recognise that the Islamic Republic was not the antidote to global capitalism, but one of its more perplexing products. Liberal capitalism and religious fundamentalism are functionally intertwined, positing each others at the fringes of contact as uncanny neighbours. No convenient opposition can be drawn between the two, despite the desperate fantasies of the post-Cold War left.

If Iran was neither an enemy nor an ally in the eyes of the mainstream left, nothing could be said of it, even as its devastated its own population. The empty space of criticism left behind by any serious socialist opposition to the Regime was, as always, filled in by violent and reckless US (and Israeli) foreign policy. US intervention once again appears as the ‘double’ of leftism: the enemy of the left that the left nonetheless requires in order to define itself.

Foreign intervention is often necessary. Any Marxist or socialist who denies this has either inherited the worst part of a cheap Stalinist nostalgia (protectionist socialism) or does not take seriously the devastating effects of global capitalism. With Iran, intervention was crucial. We cannot ‘let the Iranians take care of themselves’ as Peter Hitchens recently suggested - the slaughter of tens of thousands of Iranian protesters by its own leadership is enough to suggest that a more serious solution is required. Afghanistan and its Taliban rule is itself already a testament to the ridiculous idea that Western meddling in the Middle East can simply be reversed by suddenly letting the people ‘decide for themselves’. There is a liberal optimism that a group of violently oppressed people will somehow spontaneously make sound political choices after decades of having their freedom to think and to act denied. The true brutality of aggressive US foreign policy is that, when the US pulls out, it often leaves behind a political wasteland so devastated that the situation only worsens through the people’s own self-oppression.

The left is largely responsible for Iran's deadlock. They were silent in the long buildup to the conflict, and as always can only critique Western foreign policy after they have opened the door for it. It is easy in such a situation to perform a psychoanalytic retrogression, to reflect on the conditions of a symptom insofar as the symptom itself holds our critical discourse together. On the political front, US bombing of Iran is the perfect symptom: it is a point of performative lament which absolves us of recognising the true impasse on which the symptom is situated. The devastation of Iran is, in other words, a symptom insofar as it is the enemy within discourse that also acts as the condition of discourse.

The left were unable to confront the situation before the familiar enemy of Western foreign policy reared its head, and as always its voice of opposition comes only after the battle is largely lost. Today’s anti-capitalist movement enjoys the failure of its protests more than the protests themselves, and the silence of any socialist-leftist coalition preceding US intervention in Iran cannot be disentangled from the fact that this intervention is currently operating so ruthlessly. US foreign policy is the secret fetish of the left: whilst they hate it, they never know what to do without it. Iran is the best example of this. Instead of serious international projects of popular liberation, the left wants to wait until it is too late, to lament a situation only once it has absolved itself of the responsibility to prevent it.

Antagonisms of the Everyday: Philosophy, Culture, Politics is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

title: Why the Doomsday Clock is Ridiculous
subtitle: Privatising the Apocalypse
author: rafaelholmberg
raw_article:
Late capitalism thrives in the image of its own demise. Unlike earlier industrial capital or imperial empire politics, which functioned only by violently repressing the possibility of its own end, the global techno-capitalist system is intimately fettered to the narrative of its own destruction. Apocalypse, climate catastrophe, or nuclear fallout are no longer the obverse of political economy, but moments internally deployed by capitalism to justify its perpetual reproduction - sustainable consumerism, experiments in market regulation, and the movement of capital across nuclear enemies are testaments to the fact that the end of capitalism is itself capitalised. The postmodern site of economic appropriation has become the zone of destruction that should act as the radical exteriority of economics itself.

A (not very good) joke by Freud helps illuminate this strange logic of late capitalism, where thinking of the end (whether it is a limit, an apocalypse, death, collapse, or whatever form the end may conveniently take), we never really think about the end: ‘Having reached old age, a husband tells his wife that, “when one of us dies, I'll move to Paris.”’ The supposedly absolute limit implied by an end is inevitably relativised. In other words, the possibility of death is admitted by the husband, but in this same moment he disavows it, implying that it won't after all be his own death. The very knowledge that death exists is what paradoxically keeps us from truly confronting it. And yet the logic employed by the husband extends far beyond the confines of the joke itself or even of marriage. The husband’s position – by the very fact of thinking about death he denies his own death – reflects a problem that has plagued philosophers for centuries, a problem that is no less relevant today: how do we begin to accurately think of an end, and why does mapping the end all too often have the inverse effect of obscuring it?

On the level of economics, thinking the end of capitalism no longer acts as its negation, but rather as an internal limit which justifies its continuation. A concentrated staging of this paradox, a ‘microcosmic’ representation of the self-obscuring economy of the end of the world, can be clearly deduced if we look at the famous Doomsday Clock and its ridiculous history. Each year, ‘experts’ from The Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists’ Science and Security Board gather to decide on how far from ‘midnight’, or the end of the world, we currently are. It quickly becomes clear, however, that these intellectuals are simply convenient spokespeople elected by a liberal-capitalist establishment to represent and further its private financial interests.

The Doomsday Clock, with its arrows approaching the ‘midnight’ of a nuclear fallout, first appeared on the cover of the Bulletin in 1947, where it was set at seven minutes to midnight. In 1949, the clock jumped ahead to three minutes to midnight, following the first ever test explosions of the Soviet Union’s atomic bomb programme. The clock wavered around this mark by a few minutes for 14 years, before abruptly moving back to twelve minutes to midnight in 1963, due to the US and the Soviets signing the Partial Test Ban Treaty, which placed America in a slightly more self-certain position of global control. For nearly three decades, the Doomsday Clock showed miniscule changes, approaching and retracting from midnight by a minute or so every few years.

In 1991, prior to the collapse of the Soviet Union, the US and Russia signed the Strategic Arms Reduction Treaty, leading the clock to expectedly move back to the biggest distance from doomsday seen since its conception: seventeen minutes to midnight. There is an obvious ‘strangeness’ to the motion of this Doomsday Clock. On the one hand, it seems clear enough that every year that we do not strategically and collectively act to prevent mounting geopolitical tensions and greenhouse emissions, we are inching towards a large-scale ecological collapse. The train we are on is clearly heading towards a cliff. But if we look at the Doomsday Clock, it seems this train is freely able to move back and forth. It might move back in the year when some arms reduction treaty is signed, but this year will nevertheless have seen an unprecedented level of CO2 emissions. In other words, whilst effective insofar as it alarms the public, the clock appears largely contingent, it sees the total end of the world as dependent on its own criteria.

Nobody has seemed to recognise the paradox of an international organisation of experts, gathering on a determined yearly schedule to discuss the speculative possibility of the absolute end of any form of civil organisation or structure. On several occasions, the doomsday clock has been moved back from midnight, not because we have managed to ‘avert crisis’, but because its architects and the system they represent have briefly looked optimistically at the state of international relations. But this optimism is little more than an selective reading of the facts: things might have looked better between the US and the Soviet Union, but this had little effect on growing tensions in the Middle East or the steady progression of the climate disaster. Whatever this end is that is reflected by the Doomsday Clock, it is an end not external to, but rather internally thinkable by, today’s scientific community. We might be tempted to argue that the Doomsday Clock is simply a metaphor, or a sensationalised analysis of the possibility of nuclear war. But it has far broader implications. The clock has even been used as a hermeneutic system for trying to objectively understand the risk to human existence.[1]

In 2025, however, the Doomsday Clock was set at its most alarming position so far: 89 seconds to midnight, which is one second closer than the previous year - an intentional increment which the board used to imply that ‘every second counts’. This update by the Bulletin was accompanied by a statement that their minuscule adjustment intended to serve as an “unmistakable warning that every second of delay in reversing course increases the probability of global disaster.”[2] This plea is a familiar one, and yet the urgency of our precarious situation signalled by the Doomsday Clock – the imminence of the end, of a collapse, of a global destruction, of the end of the world – nevertheless brings to mind a recurrent antinomy: what is this end that we are being warned against? What do we make of the fact that the objective scientific committee is as unable to make sense of the end of the world as the everyday observer or science fiction reader? What do we make of the fact that the category of apocalyptic literature and film is as experimental as any scientific analysis of what the end of the world will look like? My argument here is that we need to take a step back and see the problem as even more complicated than initially expected.

The problem with imagining the end of the world is partially located in the very notion of ‘the end’ as such. The capitalist realist line therefore needs to be rethought: we can think neither the end of capitalism nor the end of the world. In fact, it is very possible that the two are intertwined, that we cannot think the end of the world (or that we can only think it in incoherent ways) because we cannot think the end of capitalism. The uneasy implication of this is that the distinction between the end of the world and the end of capitalism is a false distinction. The wish to draw a neat division between the two has the inevitable consequence of not only confronting us with capitalism or the world, but with the very meaning of the ‘end’ itself.

Two months ago, on January 27, 2026, the Doomsday Clock moved forward four seconds, now being set at 85 seconds to midnight. Between 2024 and 2025, the one-second incremental movement of the clock from 90 seconds to 89 seconds to midnight was intended to be poignant, to symbolise that “every second counts” in our approach to doomsday. What, then, should we make of these additional four seconds? If one second is intentionally poignant, are the four seconds not an accidental act of relativising this poignancy? It seems that if one second is critical, four seconds is critical enough to be self-defeatingly flippant, to only highlight that this ‘end’, the apocalyptic confrontation with disaster, is as terrifying as it is obscure. In fact, in the arbitrary use of ‘poignant seconds’ - from 90, to 89, to 85 seconds to midnight - it is clear that an opportunistic privatisation of an obscure catastrophe is taking place. Today, it is the elite establishment and global, private industry which most clearly benefits from the danger of its own demise. From sustainable capitalism, ethical consumerism, and even a politicisation of the climate catastrophe, industry and its opposite, apocalypse, have entered into an uncanny and terrifying tango. The Doomsday Clock is ridiculous precisely because it points at a new stage in capitalism: the apocalypse industry, in which the end of the world is relativised and privatised, where even ‘expert voices’ reflect the volatility and profitability of the apocalypse.

Antagonisms of the Everyday: Philosophy, Culture, Politics is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

[1] SJ Beard & Rachel Bronson, A Brief History of Existential Risk and the People Who Worked to Mitigate It, in The Era of Global Risk (Open Book Publishers, 2023).

[2] The Clock Shift, thebulletin.org.

title: How to Die Ironically
subtitle: Three Versions of Death: Heidegger, Nietzsche, Kant (& Others)
author: rafaelholmberg
raw_article:
Emerald Fennell’s “Wuthering Heights” is a remarkable parody. And yet the film has been so sensationalised, I would argue, precisely because its status as ‘parody’ is profoundly unclear. On the one hand, it parodies the tragic fate of Emily Brontë’s original novel. At the same time, it seems to pathetically stylise the parody already inherent to the tragedy of the novel itself. But the more entertaining displacement in the positions of tragedy and parody is Woody Allen’s Love and Death (1975), which tells the story of a Boris, a cowardly Russian pacifist during the Napoleonic Wars. The film is not merely a parody of the supposed ‘heroism’ of the Russian Army’s resistance to the French (Boris proudly proclaims that he will not be fighting, after which he is bullied and humiliated into enlisting) but also a broader parody of Tolstoy and Dostoyevsky. Boris and Sonja’s comedically verbose and intentionally nonsensical philosophical debates, their passive speculations on political opposition, or the absurd obstacles to their love, all act as entertaining but ultimately failed parodies of the tragic narratives of Russian literature.

Woody Allen’s problem was staging a parody which is already 150 years old. More precisely, he attempted to parody Russian tragedy-literature whilst missing the fact that parody is already integral to the logic of tragedy in Tolstoy and Dostoyevsky. The greatness of these authors was recognising that no true tragedy can be separated from a self-critical dimension of parody inherent to tragedy as such. A variety of typical scenes in Tolstoy and Dostoyevsky clearly frame the constitutive conjunction of parody and tragedy: farcically jealous husbands such as Levin in Anna Karenina; the spectacle of misunderstood customs in Russian haute société (where a mistimed interjection in a dinner conversation is as catastrophic as a declaration of war); the impossibly fast pacing between excessive pride and excessive humiliation in The Brothers Karamazov; the trivial tensions and bickering in a devastatingly failed marriage, or moral quandaries severe enough to be physically debilitating (a type of brutal mockery of psychosomatism).

If Woody Allen’s Love and Death is a failed mockery, what are we to make of the similarly fragile, but all the more important, distinction between life and death? Death appears at one time as an intimate moment posited by life itself, and at another time as a terrible exteriority, the incalculable and externally conditioned interruption of life against its will. For the biologist, the question may appear simpler. And yet asking a biologist the question, “why do organisms die?”, inevitably opens them up to endless evolutionary speculations. Wherever life and death are framed in a straightforward dichotomy, the temporal disparity between the two also arises: death itself is a later ‘invention’ of life - a product of the evolutionary problem posed by endless life or immortality. Life might arise enigmatically in the pre-biological world or through some prebiotic syntheses, but death is forced to catch up to life with the same simulacral spontaneity as the emergence of life itself. We must evolve to die, or posit death as initially unnatural.

The opposition between life and death is volatile, not only where life and death reciprocally presuppose each other, but where even the true opposition to death is not life but death itself. This problem arises in its most striking form when we focus on the cultural or human register, a difficulty already recognised by psychoanalysis: we cannot even argue that death is really death. Death becomes a type of fetishised extension of the contradictory short-circuits already constitutive of life. For Freud, the death drive is not simply a compulsion for self-annihilation, but a framing of annihilation or death as a mode of protracted, uncanny enjoyment. Life (or the ‘life drives’) subsists by supplementing itself with a repetitive and traumatic enjoyment of the ruptures which seem antithetical to life as such. The traumatic displacement of the mother-child dyad into a self-alienated, castrated form of social enjoyment (the moment where sexuality loses its object, where it reproduces the simulacral non-origin of civilisation itself) is a rejection of life (a rejection of naturalistic self-preservation) which constitutes this same life. The death drive repeats the negative contraction or traumatic rupture inherent to life, it is an inverted enjoyment of a brutal erasure of natural pleasure.

Antagonisms of the Everyday: Philosophy, Culture, Politics is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Death is, for Freud, an uncomfortable expression of life. Laplanche takes this argument to its logical extreme: death is that in life which is more than life itself. It is the alterity (or étrangeté intérieure) which is both internally constitutive of the drives as well as absolutely irreducible to them. Death is not opposed to sexuality, but rather the perverted form of excessive enjoyment of a traumatic impasse which is inherent to sexuality. Death appears, for Laplanche, as life’s relation to itself. But there is another difficulty. Both Freud and Laplanche (or psychoanalysis generally) still maintain an opposition between life and death. Instead of erasing a limit, they reveal a different type of paradox: life is structured by its opposite (death). It is true that the idea of death negating life already misses the annihilating or auto-destructive logic of death already present in any expression of life, but the psychoanalytic paradox of life-as-death still maintains a consistency to either term. Life is contradictory and complex, but it is still life - life represents an individual, and death would be the end of the individual.

It is not merely death’s opposition to life, but death’s opposition to itself, which casts an obscure shadow over death as a coherent concept. Here, I will tentatively introduce three variations on the logic of death: a Heideggerian, a Schellingian-Nietzschean, and a Kantian-Sublime image of death: death as oppositional, death as self-negating, and death as creating its own possibility.

Heidegger and Woody Allen: Life or Death

The more modest death, the Heideggerian variation, stages death as a postponed finitude. Unlike the animal, the human (or Dasein) entertains a permanent existential relation to the projection of death, to the finitude of Dasein. Being-in-the-world simultaneously implies Dasein’s being in relation to its possible non-existence. Heidegger1 insists on the immanence of being, its immediate positioning in a continuous relation to otherness, to the de-centered anti-Cartesian world of an always-existing thereness. Dasein is thrown into the existence of the other which is always there as inherent to Dasein. The problem with Heidegger’s otherness, however, is that the other cannot be framed as simply a continuation of the self, the subject, or Dasein - the dimension of alterity inherent to the other necessarily implies that it is an interruption of the self. The other is what renders the self other-than-itself. The other is a rupture of pure difference, an irreducible multiplicity of de-subjectivised enigma lodged in subjectivity itself. The only experience which does approach difference or non-identity for Heidegger is the projection of Dasein’s own possible non-existence: Dasein’s Sein-zum-Tode (being-toward-death).

Death is, for Heidegger, continuously affirmed as a structuring principle of everyday existence. We know we will die, and we embody this projected knowledge of a future impossible limit, a finitude which we will not transcend. With Heidegger, death may take the centre stage in life, but it is nonetheless conveniently (and problematically) opposed to life. The proof of this lies in Heidegger’s strange (and somewhat naive) distinction between an “authentic” versus “inauthentic” being-towards-death. There is no need for a long discussion of authenticity and inauthenticity in Heidegger (since there is not much substantial material there to be discussed in the first place). Fundamentally, authentic being-towards-death implies an acknowledgement of a future finitude, an embrace of eventual death as structuring life itself. The inauthentic alternative, on the other hand, is a type of fleeing, a concealment or turning away from the possibility of death. We are always-already in a relationship with death, and this relationship is for Heidegger either accepted or denied.

Heidegger’s being-towards-death avoids the fact that the fled from death and the embraced or avowed death are radically different things. It is very possible to both avow and flee from death. Action films are littered with depictions of a stoic who knows that he himself will die, but is devastated by the death of a partner, a pet, or a child. This duality emerges because Heidegger’s death, whilst appearing as the simple finitude of life, fails to recognise a division or self-concealment inherent to the concept of death. We return here to the problem with Woody Allen, who stages an opposition (between parody and tragedy) without realising that opposition is inherent to the thing itself. Like Woody Allen, Heidegger mistakes an internal opposition for an external one. The opposition separating life from death is, in its more radical dimension, an opposition already separating death from itself. This opposition not of death, but within death - death as self-cancelling - is foreign to Heidegger but crucial for the second variation of death, the Schellingian-Nietzschean variation.

Nietzsche and Schelling: Death Against Itself

Nietzsche stages his attack on the Idea, and more generally on philosophical reflections on the transcendent, with an unconditioned and violent ‘Act’. This Act is not simply the counterpart to abstract theorising, but an inversion of the established division between theorising and practice. The Nietzschean Act is an inversion of our moral doctrines which can only appear as unrecognisable, as a dizzying suspension of established philosophy. Beyond Good and Evil’s attack on “the philosophers”2 is not merely a dissatisfaction with the terms of their rational constructions - it is not a discontented reflection over the inconsistency of theoretical systems, but rather a lament over the aesthetic ‘ugliness’ with which ethical and metaphysical discourse was implemented. This ugliness - identified with Socrates, Christianity, idealism, and nihilism - lies in a passive justification of a deep perversion of ‘instincts’. Even a-historical theory and metaphysics are for Nietzsche always genealogical, and even great dialectical systems have inherited a pathological passivity, an abandonment to perverse and nihilistic reflection.

This philosophical Act of Nietzsche, in which (as Badiou argues) philosophy becomes irreducibly dynamic, where action and philosophising become inseparable, is possible only with a radical transvaluation of all established values. The coming noontide that will extinguish European nihilism will be so turbulent that, to our established moral coordinates, it may well arise as a violent and unrecognisable enigma. Transvaluation of values means a conceptual and practical annihilation - a violence imposed from the outside (from the ‘blonde beasts’3) or from the irreducible figure of the Übermensch. Annihilation, death, and continuation (a continuation of sublime Dionysian excess) are therefore indistinguishable in the Nietzschean Act. It is this disjunction, where life must formulate itself as death, or more precisely where death becomes incompatible with itself (where death implements a new immortal set of moral coordinates) that the Will to Power manifests itself.

Nietzsche’s Will to Power is not merely an affirmation of life, but of a specific form of death. The problem with the early, Schopenhauerian Nietzsche of The Birth of Tragedy is that the distinction between Dionysian excess and rational Apollonian sterility maintains life and death on two separate entities. With his later writings, however, the destructive tendency of the will to power becomes an auto-conditioned counter-tendency to itself. Will to power is a violent discontinuity, a rupture in the established order of things. It is an incalculable Act that arises as death, the death of the Platonic and post-Platonic order of morality and philosophy. And yet this death is not to be distinguished from the life (values, ethics, religion) that it opposes, but rather from death itself - the death already implicit to the life of European nihilism. If, according to Nietzsche, a nihilistic principle of death has already been installed as inherent to the Christian doctrine of life, then its death is never really death. More specifically, the perplexing dimension of the will to power is that it divorces death from itself. Any affirmation of death has already collapsed death into its own unsustainable interiority. It has rendered death and inarticulable and archaic object.

The will to power makes possible an event-like rupture not only of the future but also of the past. This will is a violent imposition of both equality and of difference. Logic itself, as Nietzsche argues in the collected notes of Will to Power4, is the product of an asymmetrical intensity, a forceful making-equal of purely discontinuous terms. The will to power is, in other words, a disorienting inquiry not only into life but into death. If death was programmed into philosophy as the counterpart to life, Nietzsche’s death is rendered incompatible with itself. Death as such begins to implode the distinction between life and death: life versus death is one (faulty) version of death’s disjunctive relation to itself. The very coordinates by which we frame these two moments are undone by the event of the ‘Act’, and the will to power lays the groundwork for our understanding of death as internally different from itself.

As with many philosophical ‘events’, we find that the event retroactively inscribes itself into its own past. In fact, an instance of the Nietzschean Act - the violent and forceful interruption of a recursive metaphysical continuity - had already appeared with a similar force in Schelling’s philosophy. In Schellingian terms, the image of death is rendered a negative ontological contraction, a self-obfuscating negation, which by its own ‘action at a distance’ on itself spontaneously implements the opposition between life and death. Life and death are not a constitutive opposition, but rather the expressions of an a priori recursive disjunction, a temporal short circuit where death makes itself possible by interposing life (or non-death) as an internal principle of itself. The easiest approach to this problematic is by looking at the inconsistent movement between light and darkness, both as constitutive of freedom5 (and the evil or abjection inherent to freedom) and of ‘the past’ as God’s internal rejection of Himself6.

Freedom is formulated as possible only through the auto-division of God both into what He is and what is absolutely incompatible with divinity itself. Good and Evil, both of which are necessary for any possible freedom, are not a dyadic opposition, but rather inconsistent expressions of a formal disparity of God’s relation to Himself. The coordinates of freedom are, in other words, firstly implemented by God’s gesture of self-rejection, of embodying in His identity to Himself a negative contraction which, in Schellingian terms, is both intimate to God and a vulgar denial of God, the ‘extra-divine internal to divinity’. In onto-theological terms, light does not precede darkness, nor does darkness precede light. Unlike Hegel’s being and nothing, it is also not simply the case that light and dark indefinitely and retroactively precede each other. Instead, the ground of darkness is a ground that renders it other than itself. Freedom as such is possible only because of the division not between good and evil, light and darkness, or life and death, but in the auto-division already deployed within each term.

The ontologically radical extension of this is in Ages of the World (in particular the third version of 1815), where a temporally altered avatar of the Nietzschean ‘Act’ is evident. The Platonic-Parmenidean problem of identity and difference is returned to by Schelling: specifically, does the one or the many come first? Any temporal reconstruction of the relationship between identity and difference, Schelling insists, is useless. A growing tension inevitably emerges, where something must come first, but this primacy would in turn presuppose that which this primary thing differs from. Schelling’s solution is a violent erasure of the basic terms of the one-many dyad. A blind act, a discontinuous rupture irreducible to any identity-logic, emerges as the a-temporal, impersonal division which retroactively implements identity as prior, but as a secondary (or simulacral) priority. The act is not the outcome of an impossible tension between identity and difference, but rather the condition of it, and yet a condition which emerges as a rupture within identity. Identity cannot be self-contained, it cannot form a monadic principle opposed to difference, and is instead retroactively imposed as the ‘first thing’ only after an inarticulable act or violent break from nothing.

God is forced to violently divorce Himself from his own ground, as Schelling puts it later in the text. Or rather, He is forced to posit something radically alien as intimately constitutive of His own past, so that He can emerge as the origin of what exists. God is, in other words, His own abject excess. The unassimilable residue of God is God Himself, a discontinuous enigma by which God retroactively furnished His own possibility. To put in in other terms, in order to exist, God posits His own Death. The true tragedy of our having ‘killed God’ is to discover that God had already done so Himself. In its theological dimension, death is in other words incompatible to itself. Death is always death and something else, a remainder out of which death is only superficially opposed to life. In the Schellingian and Nietzschean logic, death is not death. Or rather, death is in excess of itself. It is only via death that something else, something opposed to death, shines through.

What is clear in this progression is that death is not easily locatable. A more violent discontinuity implicit in death, a displacement of death towards itself, emerges - or an unassimilable intensity which divides death between itself and an impossible excess in the form of life. Death is the ‘third term’ between life and death. A version of death not only as dividing, but as a creative, pseudo-sustaining enigma - death as a theatre that stages itself - is what is most clearly derived from the third variation: a variation seen both in Kant and in Nick Land’s incomplete reading of Kantian sublimity.

Kant (and Nick Land): Death as a Theatre

Emil Cioran once said that “without the idea of suicide I would surely have killed myself.”7 This is not simply a pessimistic reflection on the preference of never having been born. Neither is it a type of disavowal where the possibility of doing something is what ensures that you never do it. The line clearly implements a division between suicide and killing oneself - a division, in other words, in which death acts as an antidote to itself. Death cannot simply be opposed to life. As we see with Nietzsche and Schelling, not only are ‘life’ and ‘death’ self-defeating terms, or marked by a permanent internal difference, but death itself progressively arises as a specific modality of life. Life entertains death as the latter’s modus operandi - it is through death that something is permitted to embody death as a mode of life.

In her famous treatise on destructive plasticity8, Catherine Malabou writes that death emerges not as the predictable culmination of life, but as an unexpected accident. However much we ‘know’ that we will die, the experience of death is irreducible to any expectation, as much creative as it is destructive. Death arises both ridiculously and terrifyingly as an infinite experimental variation which defeats any single ‘logic’ or acceptance of death. The Kantian element to death being discerned here is that death is a creative eruption of discontinuity both within life and as a presupposition to life. Death constructs its own prior moments, it retroactively stages the scene in which it takes place - and yet this scene never quite fits. Death is always the exception to its own logic, the irregularity within the scene it has itself constructed. This perplexing logic of death, its violent and ‘retro-constructive’ dimension, can be approached via Kant’s writings on the sublime in the Third Critique9.

Sublimity is the culmination of judgement that simultaneously arises as incompatible with judgement itself. Unlike the Schellingian sublime of the early System of Transcendental Idealism10, where sublimity reflects the imperfection of the observing subject, Kant’s sublime is an instance of reflexively imposed violence of the faculties upon themselves. Where Kant erects an intraversable gap in knowledge (between the noumenal and the phenomenal - the exteriority of the in-itself and our categories of understanding), his third critique insists that we can nevertheless judge what we cannot know. A type of disjunctive bridge (the Urteilskraft or power of judgement) is created by Kant, where an epistemological impasse nonetheless has a creative effect on the imagination. With the sublime, however, this play of the imagination is violently negated - in the sublime we glimpse something irreducible to the coordinates of the imagination. Sublimity is a type of perverse pleasure extracted from a destructive limitation to the possibilities of imagination.

This violent enjoyment of the sublime is the focus of Nick Land’s essay Delighted to Death.11. This essay insists not only on the violence of the sublime, but on the negation of the imagination by reason. Reason forcefully imposes itself on the imagination, it is a judicial excess which constitutes the de-subjectivised domination of reason over our subjective imagination. In an important section, Land considers the distinction between the mathematical and dynamic sublime in Kant: “the mathematical sublime is associated with the insignificance of the human animal, and the dynamic sublime with its vulnerability.”12 With the sublime, insignificance quickly transforms from appreciation to a threat of extinction. This is easily recognised in the sci-fi trope of discovering an alien race: at first, humanity is in passive awe of an extra-terrestrial intelligence. Very quickly, however, this awe is translated into an existential threat. That which instils wonder can just as easily enact an existential destruction of human civilisation. With Kant, this threat of annihilation is carried by reason itself, since it is reason which articulates the sublime to an imagination which is not equipped to fathom it. Reason not only frames human imagination as an imperfect contingency, but carries the threat of the erasure of this imagination. The sublime is reason’s attempted negation of the human.

“Reason is something that must be built, and the site of its construction first requires a demolition. The object of this demolition is the synthetic capability that Kant refers to as the imagination, and which he exhibits as natural intelligence or animal cunning. This is the capability to act without the prior authorization of a juridical power”13

Here we see Land’s more unorthodox reading of the Kantian sublime. Reason is constructed firstly by an act of demolition. This demolition is the sublime - more precisely, the sublime is a demolition of the imagination by reason, which in the very moment of this demolition also constitutes reason as subjectivity’s irreducibility to itself. Reason is indistinguishable from the sublime, as an act that constitutes its own justification. The sublime is the demolishing of the sensible synthesis of the imagination (the ‘free play’ of aesthetic imagination). But this act is retroactively self-constituting: sublimity is the violent immobilisation of imagination from which rationality emerges both as the consequence and the agent of this violence.

But Land nevertheless misses something about Kant’s sublime - he misses the affirmation of judgement derived from its own impasse, an affirmation which directly returns us to the third (Kantian) variation on death. The sublime is a violence done against the imagination, and therefore a violence against all earlier forms of judgement: the agreeable, the good, and beauty. But Kant explicitly states that the violence against judgement imposed by the sublime is inverted into an affirmation by judgement in the form of its assumption within itself of its own incompleteness. With the sublime, judgement persists in its own insufficiency: it is capable of judging a gap that it cannot close. Put simply, we judge something as sublime precisely because this ‘something’ rejects any judgement. For Kant, judgement is the only feature of cognition capable of this enigmatic leap beyond itself.

This is the most extreme form of what Kant calls a ‘subjective universal judgement’: in the very moment where judgement collides with the finitude of its own subjective position, it posits itself as beyond its own limit: judgement implements a universal which is irreducible to itself. The violence against judgement by the sublime instantly reverses itself into an affirmation of judgement as constructing its own impossible universal. With judgement’s subsistence in its ineradicable death with in the sublime, the most extreme dimension of aesthetics emerges: the anti-aesthetic kernel of the aesthetic. Annihilation is therefore, in the Kantian sense, paradoxically constructive of the conditions of annihilation. Violent negation not only creates the conditions in which this annihilation occurs, but implements a transgressive movement in which something (in Kant’s case, judgement) is affirmed in its own annihilation.

The Kantian variation of death emerges here: death constructs the scene in which death takes place. Death allows that which dies to continue. In a psychoanalytic sense, death is formative of the very life which denies itself in death. This is made clearer when we return to the topic of suicide. Suicide is not a denial of life - as any clinician will admit, suicide does not occur in the exterior of social reality. Suicide is instead an intimate function of the social, it defines itself by the social sphere that it only superfluously leaves behind. In the absence of a social world, suicide would be incomprehensible and even impossible.

The social and the rejection of the social (suicide) collide in a single violent rupture. In the Lacanian sense, suicide is a final, desperate attempt to establish a symbolic relation to the Other, even if this relation is nothing more than the trace of an auto-erasure. To commit suicide is to leave a mark, to leave a symbolisable gap behind, or to perform a critique of the social according to the very coordinates of the social. The violent fragmentation of life is a mode of subsistence of life itself. The paradoxical equivalent to this is seen in quantum physics: as Werner Heisenberg in his pseudo-philosophical language argues14, elementary particles fragment into themselves, they divide into what they already are. This is one of the insights which forces Heisenberg to reconsider not only the divide between language and reality, but between language and itself (it is inherent to language that it miscommunicates, that it articulates as much as it fails to articulate). In the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum physics, which in one way or another insists that the inconsistencies of quantum experiments imply an ontological anomaly within reality itself, Heisenberg recognised that we may not yet have discovered the smallest components or elementary particles of reality. As he argued even in the 1950s, it seems we are capable of dividing or fragmenting elementary particles. The problem, however, is that these products of the fragmentation of elementary particles are not ‘smaller’ elementary particles, but rather themselves observationally indistinguishable from the elementary particles that were fragmented. At the quantum level, Heisenberg argued, elementary particles divide into themselves. It is in this quantum analogy that we can recognise the Kantian-sublime version of death: through death, life fragments into an affirmation and justification of itself.

Death is the condition not only of its own event, but of the opposed life in which death takes place. With this creative and self-transgressive dimension of death, we can ultimately understand suicide as a mode of staying alive. Suicide is a symbolic inscription of the materiality of the body into an immaterial social reality. Here we have the final, Kantian variation of death: it is death which presents itself as the justification of the very scene in which the violence of death is enacted. Death is a theatre which stages its own conditions.

body.content: Emerald Fennell’s“Wuthering Heights” is a remarkable parody. And yet the film has been so sensationalised, I would argue, precisely because its status as ‘parody’ is profoundly unclear. On the one hand, it parodies the tragic fate of Emily Brontë’s original novel. At the same time, it seems to pathetically stylise the parodyalready inherentto the tragedy of the novel itself. But the more entertaining displacement in the positions of tragedy and parody is Woody Allen’sLove and Death(1975), which tells the story of a Boris, a cowardly Russian pacifist during the Napoleonic Wars. The film is not merely a parody of the supposed ‘heroism’ of the Russian Army’s resistance to the French (Boris proudly proclaims that he will not be fighting, after which he is bullied and humiliated into enlisting) but also a broader parody of Tolstoy and Dostoyevsky. Boris and Sonja’s comedically verbose and intentionally nonsensical philosophical debates, their passive speculations on political opposition, or the absurd obstacles to their love, all act as entertaining but ultimately failed parodies of the tragic narratives of Russian literature.

Woody Allen’s problem was staging a parody which is already 150 years old. More precisely, he attempted to parody Russian tragedy-literature whilst missing the fact that parody is already integral to the logic of tragedy in Tolstoy and Dostoyevsky. The greatness of these authors was recognising that no true tragedy can be separated from a self-critical dimension of parody inherent to tragedy as such. A variety of typical scenes in Tolstoy and Dostoyevsky clearly frame the constitutive conjunction of parody and tragedy: farcically jealous husbands such as Levin inAnna Karenina; the spectacle of misunderstood customs in Russianhaute société(where a mistimed interjection in a dinner conversation is as catastrophic as a declaration of war); the impossibly fast pacing between excessive pride and excessive humiliation inThe Brothers Karamazov;the trivial tensions and bickering in a devastatingly failed marriage, or moral quandaries severe enough to be physically debilitating (a type of brutal mockery of psychosomatism).

If Woody Allen’sLove and Deathis a failed mockery, what are we to make of the similarly fragile, but all the more important, distinction betweenlife and death? Death appears at one time as an intimate moment posited by life itself, and at another time as a terrible exteriority, the incalculable and externally conditioned interruption of life against its will. For the biologist, the question may appear simpler. And yet asking a biologist the question, “why do organisms die?”, inevitably opens them up to endless evolutionary speculations. Wherever life and death are framed in a straightforward dichotomy, the temporal disparity between the two also arises: death itself is a later ‘invention’ of life - a product of the evolutionary problem posed by endless life or immortality. Life might arise enigmatically in the pre-biological world or through some prebiotic syntheses, but death is forced to catch up to life with the same simulacral spontaneity as the emergence of life itself. We must evolve to die, or posit death as initially unnatural.

The opposition between life and death is volatile, not only where life and death reciprocally presuppose each other, but where even the true opposition to death is not life butdeath itself. This problem arises in its most striking form when we focus on the cultural or human register, a difficulty already recognised by psychoanalysis: we cannot even argue that death isreallydeath. Death becomes a type of fetishised extension of the contradictory short-circuits already constitutive of life. For Freud, the death drive is not simply a compulsion for self-annihilation, but a framing of annihilation or death as a mode of protracted, uncanny enjoyment. Life (or the ‘life drives’) subsists by supplementing itself with a repetitive and traumatic enjoyment of the ruptures which seem antithetical to life as such. The traumatic displacement of the mother-child dyad into a self-alienated, castrated form of social enjoyment (the moment where sexuality loses its object, where it reproduces the simulacral non-origin of civilisation itself) is a rejection of life (a rejection of naturalistic self-preservation) which constitutes this same life. The death drive repeats the negative contraction or traumatic rupture inherent to life, it is an invertedenjoymentof a brutal erasure of natural pleasure.
Antagonisms of the Everyday: Philosophy, Culture, Politics is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Death is, for Freud, an uncomfortable expression of life. Laplanche takes this argument to its logical extreme: death is thatin lifewhich ismore than lifeitself. It is the alterity (orétrangeté intérieure) which is both internally constitutive of the drives as well as absolutely irreducible to them. Death is not opposed to sexuality, but rather the perverted form of excessive enjoyment of a traumatic impasse which isinherentto sexuality. Death appears, for Laplanche, as life’s relation to itself. But there is another difficulty. Both Freud and Laplanche (or psychoanalysis generally) still maintain anoppositionbetween life and death. Instead of erasing a limit, they reveal a different type of paradox: life is structured by its opposite (death). It is true that the idea of death negating life already misses the annihilating or auto-destructive logic of death already present in any expression of life, but the psychoanalytic paradox of life-as-death still maintains a consistency to either term. Life is contradictory and complex, but it is still life - life represents an individual, and death would be the end of the individual.

It is not merely death’s opposition to life, but death’s opposition to itself, which casts an obscure shadow over death as a coherent concept. Here, I will tentatively introduce three variations on the logic of death: a Heideggerian, a Schellingian-Nietzschean, and a Kantian-Sublime image of death: death as oppositional, death as self-negating, and death as creating its own possibility.
Heidegger and Woody Allen: Life or Death

The more modest death, the Heideggerian variation, stages death as a postponed finitude. Unlike the animal, the human (orDasein) entertains a permanent existential relation to the projection of death, to the finitude ofDasein. Being-in-the-world simultaneously impliesDasein’sbeing in relation to its possible non-existence. Heidegger1insists on the immanence of being, its immediate positioning in a continuous relation to otherness, to the de-centered anti-Cartesian world of an always-existingthereness.Daseinis thrown into the existence of the other which is always there as inherent toDasein. The problem with Heidegger’s otherness, however, is that the other cannot be framed as simply a continuation of the self, the subject, orDasein- the dimension of alterity inherent to the other necessarily implies that it is aninterruptionof the self. The other is what renders the self other-than-itself. The other is a rupture of pure difference, an irreducible multiplicity of de-subjectivised enigma lodged in subjectivity itself. The only experience whichdoesapproach difference or non-identity for Heidegger is the projection ofDasein’sown possible non-existence:Dasein’sSein-zum-Tode(being-toward-death).

Death is, for Heidegger, continuously affirmed as a structuring principle of everyday existence. Weknowwe will die, and we embody this projected knowledge of a future impossible limit, a finitude which we will not transcend. With Heidegger, death may take the centre stage in life, but it is nonetheless conveniently (and problematically) opposed to life. The proof of this lies in Heidegger’s strange (and somewhat naive) distinction between an “authentic” versus “inauthentic” being-towards-death. There is no need for a long discussion of authenticity and inauthenticity in Heidegger (since there is not much substantial material there to be discussed in the first place). Fundamentally, authentic being-towards-death implies an acknowledgement of a future finitude, an embrace of eventual death as structuring life itself. The inauthentic alternative, on the other hand, is a type of fleeing, a concealment or turning away from the possibility of death. We are always-already in a relationship with death, and this relationship is for Heidegger either accepted or denied.

Heidegger’s being-towards-death avoids the fact that thefled fromdeath and theembraced or avoweddeath are radically different things. It is very possible to both avowandflee from death. Action films are littered with depictions of a stoic who knows thathe himselfwill die, but is devastated by the death of a partner, a pet, or a child. This duality emerges because Heidegger’s death, whilst appearing as the simple finitude of life, fails to recognise a division or self-concealment inherent to the concept of death. We return here to the problem with Woody Allen, who stages an opposition (between parody and tragedy) without realising that opposition is inherent to the thing itself. Like Woody Allen, Heidegger mistakes an internal opposition for an external one. The opposition separating life from death is, in its more radical dimension, an opposition already separating death from itself. This opposition notofdeath, butwithindeath - death as self-cancelling - is foreign to Heidegger but crucial for the second variation of death, the Schellingian-Nietzschean variation.
Nietzsche and Schelling: Death Against Itself

Nietzsche stages his attack on theIdea, and more generally on philosophical reflections on the transcendent, with an unconditioned and violent ‘Act’. This Act is not simply the counterpart to abstract theorising, but an inversion of the established division between theorising and practice. The Nietzschean Act is an inversion of our moral doctrines which can only appear as unrecognisable, as a dizzying suspension of established philosophy.Beyond Good and Evil’sattack on “the philosophers”2is not merely a dissatisfaction with the terms of their rational constructions - it is not a discontented reflection over the inconsistency of theoretical systems, but rather a lament over the aesthetic ‘ugliness’ with which ethical and metaphysical discourse was implemented. This ugliness - identified with Socrates, Christianity, idealism, and nihilism - lies in a passive justification of a deep perversion of ‘instincts’. Even a-historical theory and metaphysics are for Nietzsche always genealogical, and even great dialectical systems have inherited a pathological passivity, an abandonment to perverse and nihilistic reflection.

This philosophical Act of Nietzsche, in which  (as Badiou argues) philosophy becomes irreducibly dynamic, where action and philosophising become inseparable, is possible only with a radical transvaluation of all established values. The coming noontide that will extinguish European nihilism will be so turbulent that, to our established moral coordinates, it may well arise as a violent and unrecognisable enigma. Transvaluation of values means a conceptual and practical annihilation - a violence imposed from the outside (from the ‘blonde beasts’3) or from the irreducible figure of theÜbermensch. Annihilation, death, and continuation (a continuation of sublime Dionysian excess) are therefore indistinguishable in the Nietzschean Act. It is this disjunction, where life must formulate itself as death, or more precisely where death becomes incompatible with itself (where death implements a new immortal set of moral coordinates) that the Will to Power manifests itself.

Nietzsche’s Will to Power is not merely an affirmation of life, but of a specific form of death. The problem with the early, Schopenhauerian Nietzsche ofThe Birth of Tragedyis that the distinction between Dionysian excess and rational Apollonian sterility maintains life and death on two separate entities. With his later writings, however, the destructive tendency of the will to power becomes an auto-conditionedcounter-tendencyto itself. Will to power is a violent discontinuity, a rupture in the established order of things. It is an incalculable Act that arises as death, the death of the Platonic and post-Platonic order of morality and philosophy. And yet this death is not to be distinguished from the life (values, ethics, religion) that it opposes, but rather from death itself - the death already implicit to the life of European nihilism. If, according to Nietzsche, a nihilistic principle of death has already been installed as inherent to the Christian doctrine of life, then its death is never really death. More specifically, the perplexing dimension of the will to power is that it divorces death from itself. Anyaffirmationof death has already collapsed death into its own unsustainable interiority. It has rendered death and inarticulable and archaic object.

The will to power makes possible an event-like rupture not only of the future but also of the past. This will is a violent imposition of both equality and of difference. Logic itself, as Nietzsche argues in the collected notes ofWill to Power4, is the product of an asymmetrical intensity, a forceful making-equal of purely discontinuous terms. The will to power is, in other words, a disorienting inquiry not only into life but into death. If death was programmed into philosophy as the counterpart to life, Nietzsche’s death is rendered incompatible with itself. Death as such begins to implode the distinction between life and death: life versus death is one (faulty) version of death’s disjunctive relation to itself. The very coordinates by which we frame these two moments are undone by the event of the ‘Act’, and the will to power lays the groundwork for our understanding of death as internally different from itself.

As with many philosophical ‘events’, we find that the event retroactively inscribes itself into its own past. In fact, an instance of the Nietzschean Act - the violent and forceful interruption of a recursive metaphysical continuity - had already appeared with a similar force in Schelling’s philosophy. In Schellingian terms, the image of death is rendered a negative ontological contraction, a self-obfuscating negation, which by its own ‘action at a distance’ on itself spontaneously implements the opposition between life and death. Life and death are not a constitutive opposition, but rather the expressions of ana priorirecursive disjunction, a temporal short circuit where death makes itself possible by interposing life (or non-death) as an internal principle of itself. The easiest approach to this problematic is by looking at the inconsistent movement between light and darkness, both as constitutive of freedom5(and the evil or abjection inherent to freedom) and of ‘the past’ as God’s internal rejection of Himself6.

Freedom is formulated as possible only through the auto-division of God both into what He is and what is absolutely incompatible with divinity itself. Good and Evil, both of which are necessary for any possible freedom, are not a dyadic opposition, but rather inconsistent expressions of a formal disparity of God’s relation to Himself. The coordinates of freedom are, in other words, firstly implemented by God’s gesture of self-rejection, of embodying in His identity to Himself a negative contraction which, in Schellingian terms, is both intimate to God and a vulgar denial of God, the ‘extra-divine internal to divinity’. In onto-theological terms, light does not precede darkness, nor does darkness precede light. Unlike Hegel’s being and nothing, it is also not simply the case that light and dark indefinitely and retroactively precede each other. Instead, the ground of darkness is a ground that renders it other than itself.  Freedom as such is possible only because of the division notbetweengood and evil, light and darkness, or life and death, but in the auto-division already deployedwithineach term.

The ontologically radical extension of this is inAges of the World(in particular the third version of 1815), where a temporally altered avatar of the Nietzschean ‘Act’ is evident. The Platonic-Parmenidean problem of identity and difference is returned to by Schelling: specifically, does the one or the many come first? Any temporal reconstruction of the relationship between identity and difference, Schelling insists, is useless. A growing tension inevitably emerges, wheresomethingmust come first, but this primacy would in turn presuppose that which this primary thing differs from. Schelling’s solution is a violent erasure of the basic terms of the one-many dyad. A blind act, a discontinuous rupture irreducible to any identity-logic, emerges as the a-temporal, impersonal division which retroactively implements identity asprior, but as a secondary (or simulacral) priority. The act is not the outcome of an impossible tension between identity and difference, but rather the condition of it, and yet a condition which emerges as a rupturewithinidentity. Identity cannot be self-contained, it cannot form a monadic principle opposed to difference, and is instead retroactively imposed as the ‘first thing’ only after an inarticulable act or violent break from nothing.

God is forced to violently divorce Himself from his own ground, as Schelling puts it later in the text. Or rather, He is forced to posit something radically alien as intimately constitutive of His own past, so that He can emerge as the origin of what exists. God is, in other words, His own abject excess. The unassimilable residue of God is God Himself, a discontinuous enigma by which God retroactively furnished His own possibility. To put in in other terms, in order to exist, God posits His own Death. The true tragedy of our having ‘killed God’ is to discover that God had already done so Himself. In its theological dimension, death is in other words incompatible to itself. Death is always deathand something else, a remainder out of which death is only superficially opposed to life. In the Schellingian and Nietzschean logic, death is not death. Or rather, death is in excess of itself. It is only via death that something else, something opposed to death, shines through.

What is clear in this progression is that death is not easily locatable. A more violent discontinuity implicit in death, a displacement of death towards itself, emerges - or an unassimilable intensity which divides death between itself and an impossible excess in the form of life. Death is the ‘third term’ between life and death. A version of death not only as dividing, but as a creative, pseudo-sustaining enigma -death as a theatre that stages itself- is what is most clearly derived from the third variation: a variation seen both in Kant and in Nick Land’s incomplete reading of Kantian sublimity.
Kant (and Nick Land): Death as a Theatre

Emil Cioran once said that “without the idea of suicide I would surely have killed myself.”7This is not simply a pessimistic reflection on the preference of never having been born. Neither is it a type of disavowal where thepossibilityof doing something is what ensures that youneverdo it. The line clearly implements a division between suicide and killing oneself - a division, in other words, in which death acts as an antidote to itself. Death cannot simply beopposedto life. As we see with Nietzsche and Schelling, not only are ‘life’ and ‘death’ self-defeating terms, or marked by a permanent internal difference, but death itself progressively arises as a specific modality of life. Life entertains death as the latter’smodus operandi- it is through death that something is permitted to embody death as a mode of life.

In her famous treatise on destructive plasticity8, Catherine Malabou writes that death emerges not as the predictable culmination of life, but as an unexpected accident. However much we ‘know’ that we will die, the experience of death is irreducible to any expectation, as much creative as it is destructive. Death arises both ridiculously and terrifyingly as an infinite experimental variation which defeats any single ‘logic’ or acceptance of death. The Kantian element to death being discerned here is that death is a creative eruption of discontinuity both within lifeandas a presupposition to life. Death constructs its own prior moments, it retroactively stages the scene in which it takes place - and yet this scene never quite fits. Death is always the exception to its own logic, the irregularity within the scene it has itself constructed. This perplexing logic of death, its violent and ‘retro-constructive’ dimension, can be approached via Kant’s writings on the sublime in the Third Critique9.

Sublimity is the culmination of judgement that simultaneously arises as incompatible with judgement itself. Unlike the Schellingian sublime of the earlySystem of Transcendental Idealism10, where sublimity reflects the imperfection of the observing subject, Kant’s sublime is an instance of reflexively imposed violence of the faculties upon themselves. Where Kant erects an intraversable gap in knowledge (between the noumenal and the phenomenal - the exteriority of the in-itself and our categories of understanding), his third critique insists thatwe can nevertheless judge what we cannot know. A type of disjunctive bridge (theUrteilskraftor power of judgement) is created by Kant, where an epistemological impasse nonetheless has a creative effect on the imagination. With the sublime, however, this play of the imagination is violently negated - in the sublime we glimpse something irreducible to the coordinates of the imagination. Sublimity is a type of perverse pleasure extracted from a destructive limitation to the possibilities of imagination.

This violent enjoyment of the sublime is the focus of Nick Land’s essayDelighted to Death.11. This essay insists not only on the violence of the sublime, but on the negation of the imagination by reason. Reason forcefully imposes itself on the imagination, it is a judicial excess which constitutes the de-subjectivised domination of reason over our subjective imagination. In an important section, Land considers the distinction between the mathematical and dynamic sublime in Kant: “the mathematical sublime is associated with the insignificance of the human animal, and the dynamic sublime with its vulnerability.”12With the sublime, insignificance quickly transforms from appreciation to a threat of extinction. This is easily recognised in the sci-fi trope of discovering an alien race: at first, humanity is in passive awe of an extra-terrestrial intelligence. Very quickly, however, this awe is translated into an existential threat. That which instils wonder can just as easily enact an existential destruction of human civilisation. With Kant, this threat of annihilation is carried by reason itself, since it is reason which articulates the sublime to an imagination which is not equipped to fathom it. Reason not only frames human imagination as an imperfect contingency, but carries the threat of the erasure of this imagination. The sublime is reason’s attempted negation of the human.

“Reason is something that must be built, and the site of its construction first requires a demolition. The object of this demolition is the synthetic capability that Kant refers to as the imagination, and which he exhibits as natural intelligence or animal cunning. This is the capability to act without the prior authorization of a juridical power”13

Here we see Land’s more unorthodox reading of the Kantian sublime. Reason is constructed firstly by an act of demolition. This demolition is the sublime - more precisely, the sublime is a demolition of the imaginationbyreason, which in the very moment of this demolition alsoconstitutesreason as subjectivity’s irreducibility to itself. Reason is indistinguishable from the sublime, as an act that constitutes its own justification. The sublime is the demolishing of the sensible synthesis of the imagination (the ‘free play’ of aesthetic imagination). But this act is retroactively self-constituting: sublimity is the violent immobilisation of imagination from which rationality emerges both as the consequence and the agent of this violence.

But Land nevertheless misses something about Kant’s sublime - he misses theaffirmationof judgement derived from its own impasse, an affirmation which directly returns us to the third (Kantian) variation on death. The sublime is a violence done against the imagination, and therefore a violence against all earlier forms of judgement: the agreeable, the good, and beauty. But Kant explicitly states that the violence against judgement imposed by the sublime is inverted into an affirmation by judgement in the form of its assumptionwithin itselfof its own incompleteness. With the sublime, judgement persists in its own insufficiency: it is capable of judging a gap that it cannot close. Put simply, we judge something as sublime precisely because this ‘something’ rejects any judgement. For Kant, judgement is the only feature of cognition capable of this enigmatic leap beyond itself.

This is the most extreme form of what Kant calls a ‘subjective universal judgement’: in the very moment where judgement collides with the finitude of its own subjective position, it posits itself as beyond its own limit: judgement implements a universal which is irreducible to itself. The violence against judgement by the sublime instantly reverses itself into an affirmation of judgement as constructing its own impossible universal. With judgement’s subsistence in its ineradicable death with in the sublime, the most extreme dimension of aesthetics emerges: the anti-aesthetic kernel of the aesthetic. Annihilation is therefore, in the Kantian sense, paradoxically constructive of theconditionsof annihilation. Violent negation not only creates the conditions in which this annihilation occurs, but implements a transgressive movement in which something (in Kant’s case, judgement) is affirmed in its own annihilation.

The Kantian variation of death emerges here:death constructs the scene in which death takes place. Death allows that which dies to continue. In a psychoanalytic sense, death is formative of the very life which denies itself in death. This is made clearer when we return to the topic of suicide. Suicide is not a denial of life - as any clinician will admit, suicide does not occur in the exterior of social reality. Suicide is instead an intimate functionofthe social, it defines itself by the social sphere that it only superfluously leaves behind. In the absence of a social world, suicide would be incomprehensible and even impossible.

The social and therejectionof the social (suicide) collide in a single violent rupture. In the Lacanian sense, suicide is a final, desperate attempt to establish a symbolic relation to the Other, even if this relation is nothing more than the trace of an auto-erasure. To commit suicide is to leave a mark, to leave a symbolisable gap behind, or to perform a critique of the social according to the verycoordinatesof the social. The violent fragmentation of life is a mode of subsistence of life itself. The paradoxical equivalent to this is seen in quantum physics: as Werner Heisenberg in his pseudo-philosophical language argues14, elementary particles fragment into themselves, they divide into what they already are. This is one of the insights which forces Heisenberg to reconsider not only the divide between language and reality, but between language and itself (it is inherent to language that it miscommunicates, that it articulates as much as itfailsto articulate). In the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum physics, which in one way or another insists that the inconsistencies of quantum experiments imply an ontological anomaly within reality itself, Heisenberg recognised that we may not yet have discovered thesmallestcomponents or elementary particles of reality. As he argued even in the 1950s, it seems we are capable of dividing or fragmenting elementary particles. The problem, however, is that these products of the fragmentation of elementary particles are not ‘smaller’ elementary particles, but rather themselves observationally indistinguishable from the elementary particles that were fragmented. At the quantum level, Heisenberg argued, elementary particles divide into themselves. It is in this quantum analogy that we can recognise the Kantian-sublime version of death: through death, life fragments into an affirmation and justification of itself.

Death is the condition not only of its own event, but of the opposed life in which death takes place. With this creative and self-transgressive dimension of death, we can ultimately understand suicide as a mode of staying alive. Suicide is a symbolic inscription of the materiality of the body into an immaterial social reality. Here we have the final, Kantian variation of death: it is death which presents itself as the justification of the very scene in which the violence of death is enacted. Death is a theatre which stages its own conditions.
